Central to this view was the concept of sexual complementarity. Male and female bodies are anatomically and physiologically complementary in a way that same-sex pairings are not. This complementarity is not merely incidental; it is the mechanism by which human reproduction occurs and by which children are ordinarily linked to the two adults responsible for their existence. Proponents cited the consistent historical record: every society that has institutionalized marriage has done so in gendered terms, even those tolerant of same-sex conduct. The universal presumption, they contended, was that marriage is oriented toward procreation and the orderly transmission of parental responsibility.

This biological reality, they argued, grounds the state’s interest in marriage distinct from its interest in other intimate relationships. Once that transformation occurred, they predicted, it would become increasingly difficult to articulate why the state should prefer married biological parents over other configurations.

Social-science evidence was marshaled to support the child-welfare claim. Defenders pointed to decades of research showing that, on average, children fare best when raised by their own married biological mother and father. Studies cited included longitudinal data on outcomes in education, emotional health, physical health, criminality, and economic mobility.
